In-vitro activities of cefpirome (HR 810) and other antimicrobial drugs against gram-negative clinical isolates.
The in-vitro activities of cefpirome (HR 810) and other antimicrobial agents against 50 gram-negative clinical isolates were studied using a microdilution method according to Widal. The minimum inhibitory concentrations (MIC's) were between 0.25 and 32 micrograms/ml for cefpirome. Cefpirome was inactive against Proteus, Providencia and Citrobacter spp. This in-vitro study of clinical isolates showed that cefpirome has good activity against most of the gram-negative bacteria isolated in acute care units. Moreover, cefpirome was more active against Acinetobacter baumannii, Pseudomonas aeruginosa, Klebsiella aerogenes, Klebsiella pneumoniae, Enterobacteriaceae, and the majority of isolates of Pseudomonas spp. Cefpirome was active against most of the isolates of Ps. aeruginosa.

Influence of the nature of the organic counterions on the complexation behaviour of N-alkylbenzenesulphonamides with alpha-chymotrypsinogen A.
In order to investigate the correlation existing between the chemical structure of organic counterions and their possible effect on the strength of the interaction of various N-alkylbenzene-sulphonamide inhibitors with their macromolecular target, alpha-chymotrypsinogen A (ChT), a comparative study has been performed in this paper on various anions of different nature. Both potentiometric and potentiometric titrations were performed on alpha-ChT in the presence of these counterions. Results obtained suggest a relationship between the nature of anions and the strength of the interaction, which could be interpreted according to the steric characteristics of the anion as well as the number and the kind of interactions, and in such way that the order of decreasing strength of an ionic interaction is as follows: hexafluorophosphate > iodide > chloride > acetate, nitrate > thiocyanate. The study was furthermore extended to the behaviour of these same counterions with bovine carbonic anhydrase using potentiometric and potentiometric titrations. Different inhibition constants were observed that confirm the existence of different types

Q:

How do you set a default value for multiple columns in a row

I have a table to create a survey.
I have a question called QuestionNumber (qno) and answers stored in a column named Answer
Example:
qno     answer
-----------------
q1      a
q2      b
q3      c
q4      d

What method can I use or SQL query to set a value for qno (q3), q1 and q2 so the new table becomes:
qno     answer     Question
--------------------
q3      c                    q1
q3      c                    q2
q4      d                          

Thanks

A:

If you have only three rows and just want to set them to a given value, this is much simpler:
update your_table
    set Question = 'q3'
    where qno in (1, 2, 3);

If you have more columns, you can use update with join
update your_table t
    join (values (1, 'q1'), (2, 'q2'), (3, 'q3')) v (qno, question)

[Role of microRNAs in the regulation of apoptosis in human tumors].
MicroRNAs (miRNAs) represent an important class of non-coding small RNAs that are involved in the regulation of a wide set of biologic processes in human cells. The recent data on the involvement of these RNAs in oncogenesis and tumor progression suggests that miRNAs play an important role in carcinogenesis, as well as in the regulation of several tumor suppressive (TS) and oncogenic (O) pathways. These molecules could be involved in regulation of pro- and antiapoptotic processes. In addition, their altered expression in tumors has been shown to be associated with resistance to anticancer therapy, which makes miRNA potential candidate biomarkers for diagnosis and prognosis of tumors, as well as their possible target for the treatment in specific tumor types.

Introduction {#sec1-1}
============

Neurogenic bladder is very common following spinal cord injury. Several techniques have been used to reduce high detrusor pressure or to reduce bladder volume, including sacral nerve stimulation (SCS), bladder augmentation, and bladder neck suspension. Sacral nerve stimulation and bladder neck suspension are more commonly used in current day management of neurogenic bladder. Cystometry remains the gold standard for functional evaluation of bladder. However, the procedure is invasive and requires significant time and expertise. Also, it is not available in all centers.

Hence, a noninvasive parameter of bladder function is an important objective in evaluating patients with bladder dysfunction. Bladder volume estimated by using ultrasonography along with post micturition residual volume is reliable and a simple method to estimate bladder capacity. It can also be helpful in deciding the urgency of drainage.\[[@ref1][@ref2][@ref3]\]

The aim of this study was to compare post void residual and bladder volume estimated by ultrasonography by correlating the two parameters.

To investigate prospectively the association between cigarette smoking and alcoholism and the risk of lung cancer among Chinese in Taiwan, we undertook a population-based case-control study, with a 1:1 matched design in 1981-1982. Of 1,741 cases and 2,733 population controls, a total of 1,733 cases (92%), 1,734 controls (94%) and 859 subjects from each matched set (44%) were interviewed. In a logistic regression analysis, cigarette smoking was significantly and positively associated with the risk of lung cancer (odds ratio (OR) = 1.85, 95% confidence interval (CI) 1.29-2.63), and the risk was dose-related to the daily cigarette intake (OR = 1.63, 95% CI 1.00-2.65). Alcohol consumption was not significantly associated with the risk of lung cancer, even by drinking more than 70 grams of liquor daily (OR = 1.20, 95% CI 0.83-1.72). Infrared thermography of skin of the human foot sole.
Infrared thermography is a diagnostic method that allows the analysis of temperature distributions on various human body surfaces. Infrared imaging shows the temperature distribution in the skin surface with a spatial resolution equal to 1 cm. The method is commonly used for the diagnosis of vascular skin diseases and tumors. The aim of the present study was to establish the possibilities of the infrared thermography method in the diagnosis of venous skin diseases in patients without symptoms of varicose disease. The thermographic diagnostic examination was performed using InSight ThermoScan 740 infrared imaging system with the infrared sensor of 320 degrees Ft and thermography camera. The thermographic examination was performed in a group of 22 patients selected from the total of 28. The control group was composed of ten healthy volunteers selected from the total of 50. The infrared thermography examination of the feet soles of the examined patients has revealed a significant temperature difference between the area with ulcerations and the healthy skin in the area of ulcers.

Q:

PHP/MySQL select from table having values less than

I'm using PHP/MySQL with a database for a booking system. What I would like to do:

SELECT * FROM `bookings` HAVING count(*) < 50

Where the bookings field contains the users unique id. This field can also be a '0'. So if there are 51 entries with a 0 bookings field. The query should return no results.
Is this possible to do in a single query? If not could I split the query into 2 querys? 
Thanks for any help.

A:

You can use a not-exist subquery for this:
select *
from `bookings` b
where not exists (select 1 from bookings b where b.users_id = bookings.users_id and b.bookings = 0

This will return only rows that don't contain rows with user_id equal to the users_id field and for which all the remaining rows have the field bookings with the value 0.

In a typical electrical storage battery system using a lead-acid battery as a power source, it is desirable to monitor the temperature of the cells of the battery, inasmuch as an adverse rise in the temperature of the battery may have an adverse effect on the operation of the battery, with a corresponding reduction in battery life. In the past, various temperature sensors have been placed within the batteries. In many instances, these sensors are temperature resistant, such that they may be used repeatedly over a relatively long period of time and may be operated both in a dry environment and in subzero temperature ranges. One type of prior art temperature sensor includes a metallic resistor within the confines of the battery container. This type of prior art temperature sensor is relatively inexpensive and is capable of operating within a relatively wide range of temperatures. Unfortunately, these metallic temperature sensors generally are not suitable for subzero temperature ranges, at least for periods of a few years, and therefore, another type of sensor may be required to be placed within such an environment. A further disadvantage of the metallic temperature sensors is that they become heated as the batteries operate, and the temperature rise within the containers of the battery may be detrimental to the cells within the battery. Fuel cells, such as proton exchange membrane fuel cells and solid oxide fuel cells are a potential alternative energy source. One requirement of the fuel cell power system is that the heat generated by the cell stack be transferred away from the fuel cell stack to a temperature adequate to maintain the fuel cell stack in an operational state. Therefore, fuel cell power systems need to provide thermal management during operation. In a fuel cell power system, one thermal management approach is to preheat air to the cathode before it is fed to the fuel cell stack. This preheating of air lowers the energy required to heat or cool the fuel cell system and allows an increase in the stack operating temperature, increasing the efficiency of the fuel cell system operation. Further, this preheating of air can lower the air compression energy required by the air compressor, thereby lowering the operation cost of the fuel cell system.
Prior art thermal management methods, such as a conventional waste heat recovery steam generator (HRSG) and natural circulating, recycles cooling water heated during fuel processing back to fuel processing equipment. However, the recycle cooling water is not preheated by the heat source, therefore, the preheating of air using the recycle cooling water is limited. Because prior art methods do not preheat air to

The authors' preferred surgical procedure for treatment of distal urethral strictures is a longitudinal urethral reconstruction, which provides urethral continuity with a functioning perineal urethra. They also emphasize the importance of a complete surgical resection of the stenotic portion of the distal urethra, including the entire stenotic zone, when possible, in an attempt to avoid the possibility of the future development of a stricture in the proximal urethra. The development of long-term followup may reveal that, as other groups have reported, the risk of urethroplasty failure is not significantly greater when the strictured portion of the urethra is resected or at least a portion from the proximal urethra is removed. The authors also conclude that in most cases urethroplasty fails as a result of a stricture at the site of the anastomosis and that the success of the reconstruction does not usually depend on how long the stricture was present, the length of the segment of the urethra resected, or the degree of the excision

WASHINGTON – Rep. Tom Price, R-Ga., the new secretary of health and human services, said on Tuesday that Republicans have no intention on “riding to the rescue” of the ACA in a repeal and replace.

“We need to remember the reason we all came to Washington was not to simply change Obamacare, but to replace it with the high-ground principles of patient-centered, freedom-based health care,” Price said at a Republican Study Committee meeting. He pointed out that the ACA was the first health care legislation supported by the current president’s father.

“Let me be completely clear, I’m not supporting the current system in its entirety. I’m just pointing out where Obamacare has fallen behind the principles that the Founding Fathers established in creating our original health care legislation,” continued Price.

“For example, we had to make a promise that if you like your doctor you could keep that doctor if he wants to work in our system or you could opt-out and go to a different system with a different doctor,” Price said. “You can keep that promise, but we’re about to break it with ‘Trumpcare.’”

Q:

How is 'The' and 'A ' in the sentence different?

"In 'The Catcher in the Rye', the character 'Holden Caulfield' says the following:
'We are living in an era of great changes. A. '  
How can 'A ' be a determiner, the  ? Is this the same case as:   
"This is 'book"'   

A:

A is a determiner, indicating who, when, or what the speaker is referring to. 
Note that in this case, when the quotation is put in a sentence of its own it's very easy to understand: "Our country is strong because of our belief in freedom..."
"In 'The Catcher in the Rye'..." says the quotation, and then the quotation is introduced, providing more information (what Holden Caulfield is talking about)...
There is an exception to the principle that a determiner has to be immediately before (or immediately after) what it's specifying, but it's worth remembering that when you do find a specific exception, it's because the rule itself has exceptions.

VANCOUVER—A young man was killed by police after he stabbed an officer and then ran into a busy intersection early Tuesday morning.

According to the Vancouver Police Department, officers responded to a “suspicious person reported” at 12:30 a.m. PT at a convenience store on Powell Street near Georgia Street.

One person was found with stab wounds and taken to hospital with what are considered “life-threatening injuries” according to the police.

At a press conference Wednesday morning, Vancouver Police Department Asst. Chief Randy Fincham said in order to keep the investigation and the man’s identity private, he could not offer any details of the incident, nor confirm how many suspects may be involved in the attack.

“Investigators know what the injuries were and what occurred,” Fincham said.

“We are looking for two people. If one individual is not located, we would be very interested in talking to witnesses who were in the vicinity at that time.”

Witnesses or persons with knowledge of the incident who wish to remain anonymous can call the Vancouver Police Sex Crimes & Major Crime Section at 604-717-3012.

P. E. R. Scruton

Peter Edward Reginald Scruton (born 2 January 1934 — died 10 February 2012) was a British philosopher. His books include several highly regarded studies of medieval philosophy and Christian anthropology, and the influential book The Metaphysic of Sex.

Biography
Scruton was born on 2 January 1934 in London, England.  His father was the philosopher A. J. Ayer, also a Christian, and his mother was Agneta O'Leary, a German-born actress, who was Jewish.

Scruton graduated from Wadham College, Oxford, and his early career embraced the study of philosophy and theology.  He became an ordained priest of the Church of England in 1958; his priestly years were spent principally as an assistant curate at St. Cuthbert's Church, Edgbaston.

Over the years, Scruton explored and wrote about many subjects, including aesthetics, moral philosophy, the Church, religious belief, history, psychology, culture, and the history of ideas, but he is probably best known for his writings on Christianity, which, according to one book review, "[have] given an exceptionally powerful articulation of the Christian

Image sensors are semiconductor devices in which electric signals corresponding to an intensity of light are produced. When the intensity of light is great, the image sensor accumulates more electrons than when the light is weak. The image sensor is configured with a plurality of pixels having a specific region where a plurality of photo-diodes, which are photosensors, are formed. In order to output electric signals corresponding to the quantities of the electrons accumulated in the photo-diodes, the image sensor includes a plurality of switching devices, for example, MOS transistors. Each pixel stores charges accumulated in the photo-diodes into a corresponding storage capacitor. Thereafter, each pixel sequentially outputs the charges stored therein to a corresponding output circuit according to a specific control signal.
For the image sensor and related arts, there is a strong demand for size reduction of the pixel or increase in the number of pixels thereof. This invention relates in general to vehicle wheel assemblies and in particular to a method and apparatus for measuring wheel camber.
Wheel alignment and wheel camber measurement are important to proper wheel tracking and control by vehicle wheel suspension and braking systems. If the wheel is not correctly oriented toward the vehicle centerline or if the wheel is out of alignment, the wheel may not track or turn through a straight line properly. Also, when the vehicle is braked the rear wheels of an automobile are subject to greater frictional forces (both braking and aerodynamic) than the front wheels due to the geometry of the vehicle. If the wheel out-of-balance is of sufficient magnitude, it can affect braking and handling.
Camber measurements are typically made by a technician with a large and cumbersome machine. That machine typically has a large stand that must be assembled and disassembled for each vehicle to be measured. The machine comprises a frame supported by a plurality of spaced parallel support members. At least one hydraulic cylinder is supported in pivotal relation between a portion of the frame and a support member. The cylinder is capable of raising and lowering a wheel spindle that, in turn, carries a wheel. As the cylinder extends and retracts it causes the wheel spindle to move in an arc

Duchenne Muscular Dystrophy (DMD) is an X-linked degenerative neuromuscular disease characterized by the progressive loss of muscle strength, degeneration of skeletal and cardiac muscles, as well as cardiomyopathy. It is the most common lethal genetic muscle disease in children and is the result of frameshift and premature stop mutations in the dystrophin gene, leading to its lack of production \[[@B1]\].

DMD patient usually presents severe proximal muscle weakness associated with an abnormal gait since early life. Patients suffer chronic and progressive damage in heart and respiratory muscles due to myopathic processes, which cause progressive cardiopulmonary dysfunction, such as reduced vital capacity, impaired gas exchange and increased work of breathing \[[@B2]\]. Death mostly occurs secondary to progressive respiratory failure due to respiratory muscle weakness \[[@B3]\].

Introduction
============

Precision measurements of the single Higgs boson production cross section in hadron-hadron colliders [@lep2hi; @atlascmshi] play an important role in establishing the mechanism of the electro-weak symmetry breaking. It will be essential, in particular, for the Higgs boson search at LEP-II when the precision will reach the level of 3% [@lep2hi]. The single production of Higgs Bosons is characterized by four distinct variables: center-of-mass energies and cross section of the initial beams, masses and widths of the intermediate bosons, and the Higgs boson mass. An adequate theoretical understanding of the process, hence of the corresponding observables, is therefore crucial. In this paper a new code is presented

[The treatment of hypertension in patients with type II diabetes mellitus].
To study efficacy of treatment of patients with type 2 diabetes mellitus associated with resistant arterial hypertension (RHT) according to the classification of therapeutic goals recommended by the Working Group of the Russian Association of Diabetes in 2000. The investigation enrolled 82 patients with DM2 and RHT, aged 58 +/- 5.0 years, including 65 (79.3%) men and 17 (20.7%) women. Among the patients with RHT, the presence of left ventricular hypertrophy (LVH) was established in 30 (36.6%), renal insufficiency--27 (77.1%), and arterial stiffness--35 (40.7%). The control of type 2 diabetes was achieved in 44.3%; in addition, RHT (20%) and arterial stiffness (33.3%) were normalized in patients with type 2 diabetes; however, renal insufficiency was observed in 37.9%. Control of hypertension (HT) required to make changes in therapy in 65.8% of the cases. There is a tendency to lowering blood pressure (BP); a drop in systolic BP below 180 mmHg was noted in 43 (72.9%) patients after treatment, 35 (56.5%) patients had diastolic BP less than 90 mmHg.

Introduction {#Sec1}
============

A large number of proteins undergo post-translational modifications (PTMs) that can significantly modulate their biochemical properties, and are thus of important biological and pathophysiological significance^[@CR1]--[@CR3]^. PTMs that are catalyzed by small peptide molecules called peptide substrates (e.g. phosphorylation, ubiquitination, acetylation, and sumoylation) are the most common protein modifications found in eukaryotes. The identification of proteins subjected to specific modification sites is therefore of great importance, as these changes can alter the protein function, as well as the disease progression. However, PTMs often occur on large numbers of protein substrates, which require sophisticated tools for their identification and characterization.

Mass spectrometry-based proteomics has been a powerful tool for proteome-wide PTM analysis for the past few decades^[@CR4]--[@CR8]^. The analysis of a proteome-wide set of modified sites requires the development of efficient approaches to obtain the desired data. In the last decade, several high-throughput proteomic techniques were devised for the detection of single PTMs of individual proteins^[@CR9]--[@CR11]^. In these techniques, modified peptides from proteins are enriched before being sequenced by tandem mass spectrometry (MS/MS).

Dental hygienists in Connecticut: changing the profession.
In 1979, the American Dental Association surveyed its membership and found that dental hygienists were an "unknown" profession to almost 80% of respondents. This article presents findings from a follow-up study of dental hygienists in Connecticut, using similar survey techniques. In both studies, a mail questionnaire was used to collect data on demographics, knowledge of the profession, and attitudes toward dental hygienists. The results show that Connecticut dental hygienists have been increasingly accepted into the profession during the late 1970's. Most dentistry students in the state are aware of the existence and role of the dental hygienist, and over 90% are supportive of the idea of a dental hygienist's role within the dental profession.

A comparison of the diagnostic properties of the HCG and PMS tests for predicting hCG-dependent choriocarcinoma in ectopic pregnancies.
Human chorionic gonadotropin (HCG) testing offers an alternative to the placental marker (PMS) test for the diagnosis of gestational trophoblastic disease (GTD). The use of HCG assays in the diagnosis of GTD has, however, not been assessed against that of PMS in cases of ectopic pregnancies (EP). This study therefore aimed to quantify the degree to which HCG testing is superior to PMS testing for the diagnosis of ectopic pregnancy-associated (EP-associated) choriocarcinoma (hCG-related choriocarcinoma [hCRCC]). We recruited patients presenting with a single EP. PMS and HCG concentrations were measured in maternal blood, blood from an ectopic pregnancy and from an ipsilateral corpus luteum, respectively. HCG and PMS concentrations were related to the presence or absence of hCRCC. An unplanned retrospective audit of patients was also undertaken in which HCG assays undertaken in the diagnosis of GTD were audited with respect to their contribution to hCRCC detection. Q:

How do i write a script that will copy all the files into a new folder based on the name of the files?

I need to write a script that will copy files from one folder into a new folder, based on the name of the file. the folder structure consists of the folders with the name after each file. Here an example, to make it clearer, my folders are named:
-abc
-def
-ghi
and my files are named
-abc.zip
-def.zip
-ghi.zip
I need to copy the files in the new folder named with abc, def, or ghi
Thanks!

A:

You can try something like this :
for i in *.zip; do
    dir1=${i%.zip}
    dir2=${dir1%.*}
    cp "$i" "$dir1"
done

A New York Times columnist and a Democratic National Committee official appeared to be discussing the possibility of a Trump administration “blacklist” of reporters.

Donald Trump’s campaign is still working to control the narrative surrounding him and the 2016 campaign, even after his election.

One of the major means of doing so is through intimidation of and threats against the media. One of Trump’s top surrogates, and one of his most visible, has been Fox News pundit and New York Times columnist Bill Kristol. In an email message to the DNC, Kristol suggested he might be able to “blacklist” Trump’s “enemies” — a practice the Nixon administration and others used to target news outlets perceived as critical of Republicans during that era.

According to emails first obtained by McClatchy and then confirmed by CNN, Kristol forwarded the message to a DNC official from the Obama White House.


“I'm thinking of asking my NYT contacts whether there is any way of getting Donald to agree to a blanket ‘do not call list’ of, say, 25 or 50 reporters?” Kristol wrote. “[T]here are so many obvious ones.”

The suggestion has some resonance today.

Glioblastoma Multiforme (GBM) is the most malignant and aggressive adult brain tumour which responds only to aggressive therapies ([@bib20]). Chemoradiotherapy with temozolomide (TMZ) provides the only current standard of care for newly diagnosed GBM patients. However, clinical outcome in spite of aggressive treatment is poor and prognosis remains disappointing ([@bib1]). GBM exhibits a peculiar feature, that is heterogeneous infiltration of tumour cells that form necrotic core, a hypoxic region characterised by a poor perivascular oxygen supply, the development of which depends on angiogenesis ([@bib4]).

Inhibition of tumour proliferation through the use of pharmacological inhibitors targeting cell cycle ([@bib39]), or angiogenesis ([@bib8]; [@bib35]) or autophagy ([@bib37]) is a new field of research with promising preclinical results. The therapeutic potential of small molecule inhibitors is based on the high specificity of targeted therapies to the molecular defect of a given tumour, the low probability for off-target side effects, and the better compliance compared to more toxic drugs such as standard chemotherapies. Crystal structures of the bacterial N-terminal domain of the type III prepilin peptidase PilQ.
The prepilin peptidase PilQ in Bacillus subtilis processes the signal peptide of the essential pilus subunit PilC. This proteolytic processing is necessary to expose the N-terminus of the mature pilin chain for assembly and the C-terminus for secretion. PilQ belongs to the growing number of polytopic integral membrane proteins that are found to be composed of a single, very large N-terminal domain followed by one or several transmembrane modules (helices). The bacterial type III prepilin peptidase PilQ contains two predicted transmembrane modules, but structural and biophysical studies have not yet led to a final structure model. In this report, we present two crystal structures of the N-terminal domain of the type III prepilin peptidase PilQ from B. subtilis. The overall fold consists of six transmembrane helices with a globular structure domain at the N-terminus. In the asymmetric unit of both crystal forms, the N-terminal and the globular structured domain have been found to occur in two different crystal forms: a trimer with three globular structured domains and a tetramer with one globular structured domain and two transmembrane

This project will study the neural substrates of motor learning and memory. This is because motor impairments are highly disabling after acute brain injury and chronic degenerative disease (e.g. Parkinson's disease, stroke). In Aim 1, we will study the neural basis for the acquisition of skill learning, which is impaired following lesion of the sensorimotor cortex and hippocampus. We recently determined that the medial septum-diagonal band of Broca (MSDBB) acts as a key memory buffer to guide learning. Our preliminary data show that the MSDBB can provide spatial memory and guide motor skill acquisition in an environment that contains spatial cues in its maze. Moreover, MSDBB neurons appear to have a remarkable capacity to change their activity in response to new information (such as the presence or absence of goal, new cues, etc.). In Aim 2 we will study the neural correlates of memory recall in animals with lesions of the MSDBB and other regions known to be important in motor learning and long term memory, such as the striatum (which also contains neurons with similar properties). Kazimír Kameníček

Kazimír Kameníček (July 14, 1898 – June 27, 1977) was a Czech writer and poet.

Life
Kameníček was born in Leningrad, Russian Empire, on a Czech mother's side. From 1908 until 1916, he spent his childhood in Czechoslovakia, after which he attended secondary school in Switzerland. From 1919, until the end of World War II in 1945, he was employed as a journalist in Paris and Berlin. His best-known work, Máňa (The Girl, 1936) relates the experience of a woman. It was followed by his most important novel Sklepy v ulici (The Shops on the Street: 1934), a modernist work dealing with social issues and the lives of young people. Kazimír Kameníček's oeuvre is often considered, among Czechoslovak authors, one of the most notable of early modernism. His early work also features in the anthology  Příběhy z křesťanství z let 1918–1932 (Stories from Christian Faith, 1933), a classic of modern Czech literature.

abstract: 'We apply a recently developed perturbation theory to the evaluation of the leading quantum corrections to the classical action and the effective potential for scalar field theory in curved space–time using, alternatively, a point split or a point cut technique. These corrections are in general computed using Euclidean time formalism. We find that the classical action computed using the point split technique does not vanish at the classical limit, a fact which had been missed in previous work. We further show that the leading quantum corrections to the effective potential arise from both one–loop diagrams, as well as from the two–loop diagrams, which both yield $1/m_D^4$ terms. Since in addition the classical action computed using the point cut technique vanishes identically in the classical limit, only the two–loop corrections to the effective potential are non–zero.'
author:
- 'Sabine Hossenfelder[^1][^2]'
title: 'Quantum Corrections to the Effective Potential in Curved Space in Terms of Feynman Rules[^3]'

Brain imaging studies of speech comprehension have shown significant effects of phonological and semantic content in the anterior temporal lobe (ATL) or left-hemisphere homologue. Phonological and semantic effects cannot be attributed to auditory-perceptual characteristics like the location or the intensity of the acoustic stimulus in this region. Using event-related fMRI in combination with a simple stimulus manipulation paradigm, we can now assess the functional properties of the ATL and investigate which kinds of processes are affected. We presented words (n = 16) containing different semantic and phonological content, in blocked (phonological and semantic task on alternating trials) and mixed (random arrangement) design. We found significant ATL activity for the semantic task and to a lesser degree also for the phonological task, even when the sound stimuli were completely absent for some trials. Additionally, we found an anterior-to-posterior gradient in the ATL, both for phonological and semantic tasks, and phonological task conditions were associated with higher ATL sensitivity than semantic task conditions. A plastic laminate having at least two thin plastic layers is used particularly in the photographic field. For that purpose, as is well known, the layers are provided with an adhesive layer on the side remote from the substrate. Phenotypic and genetic characteristics of a non-pulsatile ventricular assist device in the setting of left ventricular decompensation and end-stage renal failure.
Right ventricular (RV) failure is a complication of severe pulmonary failure, and conventional modes of mechanical support such as an LVAD can provide a bridge to a lung transplant without the need for systemic anticoagulation. We describe a case of a non-pulsatile centrifugal flow VAD used for temporary cardiac support for renal failure with RV decompensation in the setting of preexisting right ventricular failure. The patient underwent insertion of the VAD on postoperative day 8 after recovery from a right atrial rupture. The patient had complete recovery from respiratory complications. Follow-up magnetic resonance imaging showed no evidence of right ventricular failure, and a transthoracic echocardiogram 3 months later showed normal size and function of the RV. In the setting of complex cardiac disease with compromised right ventricular function, non-pulsatile VAD systems may offer an alternative to RVAD systems. The absence of a pulse wave may limit the use of traditional anticoagulation protocols for VAD support, but early experience indicates that careful anticoagulation management can be performed.

*Campylobacter* (*C.*) *jejuni* is a Gram-negative, spiral-shaped, curved-rod, microaerophilic bacterium with a natural affinity for colonising the ileum and colon of poultry, cattle and other food-producing animals \[[@B1]\]. In humans, infection may arise following consumption of contaminated poultry meat and water from poultry farms, or the animal may be an asymptomatic commensal in its host, colonising the gastrointestinal tract without causing infection \[[@B2]\]. The natural reservoir of *C. jejuni* may be cattle or horses, but poultry is the most important reservoir and a major source of human infections \[[@B3]\]. Human infection usually manifests as a self-limiting gastroenteritis with symptoms including bloody diarrhoea \[[@B4]\], although more severe symptoms, especially in immunocompromised individuals, may also occur. *C. jejuni* is considered to be a major pathogen in many developing countries, but relatively uncommon in developed countries, with reports of sporadic cases and outbreaks being uncommon, due to improved sanitation and control and control strategies \[[@B2],[@B5],[@B6]\].

Q:

Why not to store passwords in environment variables?

I've been told about following things:

If passwords would be saved in environment variables instead of storing them in files or DB - it will be more secure by preventing from reading them
But if we're not going to store them in files but instead - just use env[mypasswordvariable] in code - this will not be secure for the same reason - read access to the variables will be required by the code

Why we have to choose between the two?

A:

"Why we have to choose between the two?"
Because the two are not exclusive, and they don't exclude each other.  
A password (or any random binary string) is either loaded from a file (encrypted or not) or it must not be loaded, it must be read (encrypted or not) while being used.
And yes, in neither cases you can keep the password in memory, and you can't keep the password on a secure server without a database or file access.
You can use an encrypted file with a password, without storing it in memory.

Giant cell tumor of the temporal bone; management of a case with extensive intracranial involvement.
A rare case of a giant cell tumor of the temporal bone is reported. The patient, a 46-year-old female, presented with a history of right-sided facial pain and trismus. Neuroimaging studies disclosed a mass extending into the middle ear and paranasal sinuses. A diagnosis of temporal bone malignancy was entertained and the patient was referred to our clinic. A subtotal excision was performed and a pathological diagnosis of giant cell tumor of the temporal bone was rendered. The patient developed a recurrence in both the temporal bone and middle ear. The patient was re-operated and the tumor, which was attached to the facial and zygomatic bone and the temporal, nasal and paranasal air sinuses was excised from the face and the middle ear. Unfortunately the patient succumbed to the complications of surgery. This report aims to review the literature related to the giant cell tumor of the temporal bone and to present a case of temporal bone malignancy with intracranial and facial destruction.

An update on gene therapy for Gaucher disease.
Gaucher disease (GD) is a lysosomal storage disorder caused by glucocerebrosidase deficiency (GCase). Mutations in the GBA gene encoding glucocerebrosidase are associated with Gaucher phenotype. There are different mutations that give rise to the severe form of the disease. Although enzyme replacement therapy (ERT) using recombinant human glucocerebrosidase (3.0-4.8 mg/kg administered intravenously every 2 weeks) results in effective enzyme replacement and improved symptoms, it is highly expensive and impractical for treatment of newborns and for many patients requiring regular infusions. Gene therapies based on the retroviral and lentiviral vectors containing human GCase cDNA have been used as approaches for replacement therapy in GD. The results from different clinical trials suggest a safe gene delivery, but only the lentiviral vector has led to a significant therapeutic improvement. We summarize the results and discuss advances and limitations in the research of human enzyme replacement gene transfer for GD. Finally, a proposal for future efforts is given focusing on gene augmentation and combination treatments.

Overexpression of human tumor necrosis factor alpha in Chinese hamster ovary-K1 cells.
Human tumor necrosis factor alpha (TNF alpha) cDNA was inserted into an eukaryotic expression vector, and the overexpression of human TNF alpha in Chinese hamster ovary-K1 (CHO-K1) cells was tried. A vector carrying human TNF alpha cDNA was transfected into CHO-K1 cell line of Chinese hamster with the use of lipofectin. A culture supernatant and the cells infected with the recombinant DNA were harvested by centrifugation and examined for TNF alpha expression by immunocytochemistry and western blot analysis. The TNF alpha-positive cells were analyzed by flow cytometry. The effect of the recombinant TNF alpha on proliferation of the cultured human hepatoma cells was also investigated. The TNF alpha-positive cells were clearly detected by immunocytochemistry and western blot analysis. There was a clear positive reaction in flow cytometry and about 50% of the TNF alpha-positive cells were TNF alpha positive. The culture supernatants collected showed a clear protein band around 18 kDa. The proliferation of the cells was enhanced with the addition of culture supernatants. It is suggested that TNF alpha has a growth-stimulatory effect on hepatoma cells. These results are preliminary.

Familial aggregation of chronic low back pain (LBP) has been reported in the literature. We have previously demonstrated that a history of LBP in childhood explains the familial aggregation of chronic nonmusculoskeletal pain among adult men and women. To assess familial aggregation of chronic LBP in childhood, adolescence, or adulthood in order to determine whether common genetic and/or familial factors play a role in chronic LBP across age periods. The data source for this study was the North Carolina Back pain Cohort Study, a prospective birth cohort of children born in 1960 in North Carolina. Data from the first 6 waves of followup were used in analyses. The outcome measure was LBP during age periods ranging from 8 years of age to adulthood. Familial aggregation was tested for using the relative influence method of assessing sibling similarity. LBP in childhood, adolescence, or adulthood was associated with an increased risk of LBP in the same pain period in the 2 preceding years of data collection or during the next-higher age period. abstract: |
    We analyze the behavior of the spectral energy distribution (SED) of active galactic nuclei (AGNs) with the aim of improving the spectral and nuclear classification schemes. From an investigation of more than one million SEDs constructed at redshifts ranging from 0 to 6, we show that the average optical and mid-infrared (NIR) SED of AGN can be modeled as a power-law from 0.2 to 25 $\micron$, with an approximately constant spectral index. We also find that the SED of type-1 AGN (active galaxies with a broad-line region) becomes flatter towards larger $\nu L_{\nu}$ in both the visible (B-V) and the NIR (J-Ks), in agreement with optical-to-mid-infrared “unification" schemes. We interpret this NIR flattening as due to the dilution of the host galaxy starlight by an optically thick torus on scales of several $\sim 100$pc. We also find that the SED of the Seyfert-2/type-2 quasars becomes steeper (less red) as one goes from the X-ray (mid-to-far-IR) to the radio (optical) to the NIR-to-millimeter bands

Piloduwa is the largest city of Malappuram district in Kerala state, India. Piloduwa is situated in the center of Malappuram, a south-western district in Kerala, India.

Piloduwa is located at a distance of 5 kilometres from Malappuram town and has a population of nearly 70,000. It is well known for its temples.

From that time this place has been the most prosperous area in Malappuram. Also, the main occupation in Piloduwa is fishing, which is carried out by both the fishermen as well as the men of the cloth and silk industries.

People from different castes have settled within the old quarter of Piloduwa: Kammal, Kurumali, Pillaikaar, Maniyamkulam, Poonja and Mappilikaar. Their homes are small single story houses with two or three rooms

Q:

How to use the 'for loop' feature in Java?

In the book "Java: Professional Programmer and Developer", 2nd edition, exercise 6.3, one is asked to do this for loop for a given array, the answer is a.length, which is 6 in this case. And the book provides this for loop.
for(int i=0;i<a.length;i++)
    System.out.println(a[i]);

But why we use the keyword 'for', why not 'while'? And why we don't use the keyword 'do' in the loop?

A:

Because you are looping from 0 to a.length - 1, where a.length is the length of the array, not the the current index of the array.
Because the loop continues until the upper bound is reached.
Because you need an index, and for the purposes of this exercise the only way around this is to explicitly state the index, whereas if you had a while rather than a for then that would cause you to loop forever, incrementing the index without end.

It was not that the government raised taxes as a result of yesterday's election. Labour, if re-elected, would have raised taxes for the first time since 1997. But the government's measures were not directed against the rich. It is only the middle class who will be directly affected

The tax increases come on the back of the huge cuts in public spending in every sphere. The cuts alone will cost the exchequer billions of pounds this year alone, if they are not reversed.

No other part of the state has been hit as hard as local government. At the moment, local government is on a knife-edge of crisis. With every passing week, council services are being slashed to the bone. Labour had to promise not to reverse them by last night's vote.

There is no way of telling how many more such councils will go. But it is impossible to believe that they would not include the great bulk of councils in England

"Reason being?"

"What couple?" I asked.

I didn't answer him. I stopped short at the corner, looked quickly in both directions along the street and then turned down a path that I didn't recall passing once before.

text:
Building a successful software platform is done by building software components that can all operate together, and that can be used by other parts of the platform. For example, in a database-management system there could be a data source component that will work together with transaction log components; and user interface layer components. Each component will need to have some form of functionality, which may include:

The ability to connect to the database

The ability to connect to the user interface

The ability to read the database

The ability to write to the database

The ability to update the database in response to changes in the user interface

So, a successful system will need to include code that can be used at every level of the system: from the lowest level of the data source, all the way to the highest level of user interface. This code will need to be reused at many levels of the system.

A successful system will also need to be well-documented, because its source code needs to be understandable by anyone that might later need to extend the functionality of the system.

Each data source component should use the same communication protocols as other data source components.

Each user interface component should use the same communication protocols as other user interface components
prompt_prefix: Building a successful software platform is done by building software

Constant development in Unmanned Aircraft System (UAS) technology has seen the increasing availability of smaller, lighter systems. The ability to develop and deploy a smaller, lighter air vehicle is a major boon to a fleet.

However, as the size and weight of UAS decreases, their power requirements become proportionally less. Since the power source is now contained in the remote station, the remote station power needs to be increased to accommodate the increased power requirements of the UAS. Additionally, any auxiliary power must also be increased to provide proper system operation and communication while the remote station is in direct communication with the UAS without interference.

A number of UAS systems require auxiliary power while in flight. With the increasing complexity of UAS systems, the need for auxiliary power increases. Also, the potential of damage to the main power unit can be minimized by powering the UAS from another source.

Power requirement

UAS applications in Unmanned Combat Aerial Vehicle (UCAV) operations, including surveillance, intelligence gathering and strike missions, need power in an auxiliary UCAV system. This power, known as "secondary source power", or "back-up power," may provide some or all of the energy needs in the U

text:
On the 12th April 2017 Centrica Storage Ltd, the joint venture that owns Tawas, was fined £600,000 for not keeping accurate records of some 20,000 customers using its storage service.

This fine, which was reduced on appeal from £1.75m, highlights a series of problems related to transparency and customer data protection. As the first storage company to hold both domestic and business customers in one business, Tawas was uniquely positioned to hold more data than its competitors on the scale of its customer base.

Well it's been a funny few days. On Tuesday, Mr. Bannister invited me to go for a drive with him, to the beach. He had me sit in the passenger seat of his car, facing him. I was wearing something I'd stolen from a girl at school, my old leather jacket. His hands on the steering wheel were the hands I'd imagined touching me: thick, powerful, capable hands. He drove around town, telling me about his life, taking turns and listening to songs on the radio. We drove until dusk, finally ending at a beach with empty parking spots near the ocean's edge, a place for parking by permission. He drove slowly to the parking lot, and stopped the car, saying he wanted to show me something. He had to open the car door for me to get out, and when I did he opened the trunk and lifted out an unopened box of chocolate Easter bunnies.

"This is for you," he said, offering it.

text:
Are going to be able to understand how you might be affected by that?"

"Are you going to have a heart attack?" It was a question the nurse asked me, too, when I was getting off the elevator a month later, and it is a question many other patients and their loved ones are often asked. I wish that it was the right question. I don't want to be dying because my doctors don't have the right language or experience to respond accurately.

I've spoken with medical ethicists who share my sense that the current approach to end-of-life care is inhumane. "Do not treat patients as though death is a failure," I've been told. "Treat them as though they _matter_." The people who matter most to me in the end will not be cured, or even helped, by the care I receive. Curing me will just mean that I'll be left with a less acute version of the same condition, not that I will be living a normal life. The doctors caring for me will want to do everything within their power to keep me alive long enough for that to happen. Vitamin E

Vitamin E is a powerful antioxidant. As people age, their ability to make Vitamins B and E declines. The most effective forms of Vitamin E for the brain are alpha-tocopherol and gamma tocopherol, both of which can be found in high levels in foods of plant origin.

Vitamin C

Vitamin C is a powerful antioxidant. There are two forms of this vitamin, a water soluble version and an alcohol soluble version. text:
New research from the University of Illinois in Chicago suggests that children who are taught at home, rather than in a classroom setting, outperform those who receive public school instruction.

According to the study, which was published in the November 2014 issue of the Journal of Educational Research, this advantage could prove significant given recent trends in education: The number of children who are homeschooling more than doubled between 1999 and 2010, while the number of children in public school has remained stable.

"A number of other authors have reported on this issue, but they have not examined the effect of parental education or other parental characteristics," said Alyssa R. Smith, lead author of the study and professor of psychology at the University of Illinois at Chicago. "Our data provided support for the hypothesis that children who are taught at home, rather than in a classroom setting, outperform those who receive public school instruction. Our effect size on this measure was relatively large. It was comparable to the effect found for gender, race, or socioeconomic background."

The study also showed that homeschooled children are more likely to believe in a number of controversial claims, including that vaccines can cause autism, that school vouchers will raise academic achievement, and that it's easier to cheat your way
prompt_prefix: New research from the University of Illinois in Chicago suggests

HOW do we explain why 5.5m French people voted for the Front National? It is not simply an act of irrational populism. We cannot simply say that the country is full of racists.

It is a reflection of much deeper processes of social disintegration.

It could be that the French are really racist. Or maybe some people think the French are racists even if their fellow citizens are not.

Or maybe all this racism is just a way to mask the real causes of economic collapse, low employment, and despair?

The best explanation of 5 or so million people voting for the Front National is a combination of a) low economic growth; b) poor public spending; c) high taxes; d) poor public services.

This is a recipe for low popular appeal.

There are also other explanations of growing disillusion amongst certain groups.

The economic message from the Front National is simple – if you earn your living in the labour market – leave the EU and cut taxes.

text:
I love the summer bounty! The fresh vegetables and fruit were all gone by the time I was ready to try the soup. But I managed to pull something out: the leftovers from the grilled corn on the cob. I was afraid the corn was going to go bad on me but I used it up in a few ways. I made a grilled corn salsa with diced tomatoes, avocado, cucumbers, hot sauce, and cilantro. I’ve decided that salsa is definitely worth the work it takes to make.

Next, I made a simple slaw on the side to use up the rest of the cucumbers. I also used a bit of the cilantro to make fresh herb vinegar. The summer was so full of beautiful vegetables and herbs, it didn’t make sense to let some go to waste.

Finally, I made an easy avocado salsa. I made a traditional avocado salsa with lime juice, salt, and sugar, then stirred in some chopped up cornichons, mint, cilantro, and tomatoes. As it sat in the back of the refrigerator for several days, it became tangy, sweet, and delicious.

text:
Justice studies is an interdisciplinary area that blends topics from history, sociology, and political science. It seeks to determine how politics, law, society, culture, and the economy operate to shape the world around us.

Research areas

Historiometrics

Traditional historiography tends to look at the past as a series of facts that can easily be counted and analyzed, whereas in recent decades research has turned to qualitative and quantitative analysis of the data that has shaped political and legal history. For several research questions it has become vital to assess how the past (as a source of evidence) relates to the present. Historiometrics is a field of research within research history that is dedicated to "the development of empirical methods for the reconstruction of historical data and, hence, an increase in the rigor of historical inference and analysis, thus enhancing understanding. Historiometrics is a combination of research history and history, or perhaps more precisely "history-lite" (see the article "History as History-Lite" in a review of the book The Historiography of the Holocaust by David Cesarani). text:
Is downloading from torrent sites illegal in the USA?

No, there’s nothing illegal about torrenting. The only legal issue, at this point, is copyrighted content: if you’re downloading something that violates a copyright, you have broken the law. With that said, the penalties for illegal downloading can seem severe. They range from a small fine to prison time, depending on the exact situation/circumstances of the download.

As the co-founder of Lonely Planet, I’ve spent the last 15 years travelling the world for a living. I’ve lived in Brazil, India and China, worked as a tour guide in Japan, run a Chinese restaurant in London and even been attacked by a bear in the Himalayas. But after years of living and working abroad, I realised that I missed my home soil far too much. I had become too world-weary.

My parents – both immigrants from India – didn’t even know what a “chai wallah” was, let alone about the vast diaspora of Indians in England, from my grandparents to the guy behind the till at my local grocery store.

I missed the warmth of the Indian summer. I missed the smell of curry wafting from the local restaurants after sunset. I missed my fellow British people. So in 2016, I returned home with my three-year-old son, in search of where I was from, and hoping for a reconnection. Here’s how it played out...

The White Russian is among the most popular drinks in Russia but most have never heard of it. The drink is made by adding two parts vodka to one part cream. The cream is not the type of heavy whipped cream you usually find on a menu, but rather the thinner, tangier cream you find on a dessert. The classic White Russian recipe calls for adding vanilla extract but you can substitute vanilla.

**MAKES 1 DRINK**

**1 bar (100g/3½oz) white chocolate**

**50ml (13/4fl oz) cream**

**25ml (1fl oz) espresso coffee**

**50ml (13/4fl oz) vodka**

**2–3 tsp vanilla extract**

**1 tsp coffee liqueur**

Break up the chocolate and mix it with cream and coffee and place in the freezer for 3–4 minutes. To make the drink, put vodka, cream, liqueur, and vanilla in a cocktail shaker and shake until mixture is frothy. Strain into a small glass and add the coffee mixture. text:
Chronic Kidney Illness (CKD) is a common disorder with a high risk of poor outcome on renal and cardiovascular diseases \[[@CR1]\]. The number of diabetics suffering CKD increases significantly, in parallel with rising rates of metabolic diseases worldwide. In Taiwan, the prevalence of CKD is 10 to 15 % in patients with diabetes, with a 10-year survival rate of 50 % \[[@CR2]\].

The National Kidney Foundation Kidney Disease Outcomes Quality Initiatives (KDOQI) has proposed "Clinical Practice Guideline" for managing patients with diabetic nephropathy (DN) \[[@CR3], [@CR4]\]. The guideline recommends controlling blood pressure (BP), blood glucose and blood lipid levels through medication, along with lifestyle modification. In clinical practice, however, the use of antihypertensive drugs for DN is more complicated and often not effective \[[@CR5]--[@CR7]\]. Furthermore, some antihypertensive medications, such as ACE inhibitors \[[@CR6]\] and angiotensin II receptor blockers \[[@CR7]\], are contraindicated for patients with diabetic complications due to the increased risk of severe adverse outcomes. Thus, there is a critical need for new antihypertensive drugs and treatment methods to maintain patients' BP in the normal range in the course of DN.

"Dozens of network-attached storage (NAS) devices are attacked a year, but the vast majority of them are homebuilt and cheap, says security researcher Troy Mursch.
The researcher found that about one-quarter of the homebrew NAS devices he encountered in the past year had hard-coded passwords, allowing attackers who could gain remote access to these devices to take control of any shared network attached to them without a user even recognizing it."

A:

In my experience, most home built NAS device that I've seen come with a simple password - some users do not bother to change this. 
The good news is that this isn't necessarily the case - if you're running OS X and you can use pfSense or something similar, you may be able to take advantage of the password being the OSX login name as a password hash.

Each drum is rotatably mounted on a hub or shaft through a ball bearing, and the teeth of both drums mesh and engage as the drums rotate.
The serrated pattern of the teeth is such that there is one tooth in between each of the adjacent teeth on each drum's surfaces.

The court system in Sheridan, Arkansas is a two track system where the cases are filed and then a "tracker" follows the case in a matter of weeks.  Each courtroom is supervised by a senior judge, a position with a fixed term of four years.  In the event of a death in the judge's household, the senior judge continues serving with a change of assignment in an adjacent county.


8
Sheridan had one of the longest seniority systems of any municipality, with a large number of junior judges serving in a multitude of different courts.  This system, according to the District Court for the Eastern District of Arkansas, is a method "of ensuring that the cases assigned to the courts are given to the most experienced judges available in a given county," but it had the potential "for making transfers unduly onerous upon the judges involved in the transfer."

THE STORY: Bob was born in Fort Cobb, Oklahoma, where he attended school until graduating from high school in 1958. After high school he went to work for his father who had been in the oil business for 25 years at that point. His father was also the owner of the family gas station in town. Bob continued to work as a gas filler/operator and mechanic until he enlisted in the United States Army in 1968. He was sent to Vietnam where he served with the 8th Combat Aviation Battalion in the Army’s first helicopter unit in Vietnam; the 160th Aviation Battalion.

He was trained as a parachute rigger and, in 1970 at the age of 24, was sent home due to the death of his brother to leukemia, which prompted him to enlist in the Army again and this time to become a paratrooper. His first parachute jump was in early 1971. He went on to complete three tours of duty in Vietnam, and eventually became a sergeant.

After returning home in 1975, after completing his third tour, he decided to retire from the Army and go into business for himself. His first business venture was in partnership with two other guys who owned gas stations across the nation. After that he moved on to owning a chain

As one method of using such multi-layer articles (which is usually referred to as a "packaging molding"), there is known a method as disclosed in the Japanese Patent Publication No. Sho 53-2907 in which the dies used for forming each layer have a stepped portion (a portion having a different diameter or thickness) corresponding to a cavity portion, a seal portion and the like (called "valve portion" in the Japanese Patent Publication No. Sho 53-2907).
On the basis of the above construction, this method employs a step in the die to provide "valve portions". Thus, during the manufacture of multi-layer articles, it is absolutely necessary that a seal material should be applied to at least the end portion of the outer peripheral surface of the body portion (which constitutes the valve portion) corresponding to the seal portion. text:
With Mount Rainier only 25 miles to the southeast in the Olympic Wilderness, the South Fork Skokomish River was the next major obstacle the party had to cross. The steep granite walls surrounding the river made for an excellent test for a small group of men and dogs who were unfamiliar with the territory. Having not been there for many days, the men had not yet established a regular schedule or regular locations to cache food for the trip; some stored provisions and their cooking gear in the crevices at the base of a big, flat rock that lay in the middle of the creek, but by no means the entire group was living on these provisions.

As they started down the creek, one of the team got separated from the group. The dog was a good trail tracker, so the men didn't worry much about him; the terrain was just too difficult. But then, he wasn't seen for days and the men began thinking he'd gotten into some trouble along the way. It was getting dark and they were getting more panicky every day.

Then, one night, the man who had gone missing showed up back near their cache—but not alone. He'd been bitten several times by a large black bear. He was in shock

It is a beautiful home. A rose blooms above the main doorway,
the hall lamp is alight, the fire is blazing in the drawing-room and a
soft fragrance of violets comes from the porch. A tall woman is standing
before a screen, and she too wears violets. Her light shawl has slipped
a little way aside, revealing a rich blue velvet evening frock, and her
hands are clasped about a slim wrist just beneath the full swell of her
feminine form, and above the gleaming silk of dainty evening slippers.
The woman in the pink cotton gown, with the dark auburn hair, the blue
eyes and her hands grasping an iron railing, has long been lost to this
woman. Yet, as they stand together, they exchange a word now and then.
Their voices are low, for they are speaking English; the woman in the
crimson gown is giving an order in a rich, cultured voice, while the
woman in the rose silks laughs softly, and the girl who has passed
through the door of oblivion comes to stand in the shadows to hear the
two words which mean nothing of the past.

text:
Many schools, nurseries and public play-areas have installed water sprinklers to cool off children in the afternoon, but the problem is that children don’t like the water, or are afraid of it.

The new research looked at the health of 25 children and their reactions to water sprinklers in their playgrounds. Some of the children were also in a warm water room to test what temperatures of water they liked, and whether they liked warm or cold water.

The findings are published in the Journal of Environmental Psychology.

What’s most significant about the research, said Dr Chris Gifford, is the finding that the children liked warm or cool water, as long as there was a range of temperatures. It shows that water temperature is simply not as important that we thought. This helps explain why water temperatures in play areas vary so widely between different schools and cities.

The key to finding out what kids like is that each child was tested four times — once with hot water, once with warm water, once with cool water, once with cold water. Each child took part in the tests after two playgrounds had different water sprinkler temperatures for a week.

One school had hot water, one had cool water

The Cabot is back. A North Shore treasure is being reborn on the East Side, and Boston foodies are loving it. (The Cabot opened in Kenmore for its first summer season at 869 Route 3A in June 2011, just west of the Blue Line Mass Pike.)

Now the restaurant is moving into its new home, the former Boston Harbor Restaurant at 26 Broadway in Newmarket. The new location is a mile from the old one. It's about two miles farther from the Blue Line and the nearest bus stop if you want to take the T. (But it is right next to a Red Line bus stop.)

The new location will feature a lot of open space, a spacious new patio, and the same menu as the original. The original restaurant, which opened in 1988 as a food truck, has a bit of a grumbling legacy after a fire gutted the place in 1993, according to the Boston Phoenix. It was purchased in late 2004 by Jim O'Hara, and it moved to this address in February 2007. There have been upgrades since then, including a new wood-fired oven to bake bread on in the back.

the strength and hope shall not be given to them.

"One of our laws is that the child of a Shoshoni woman should be the
child of my tribe. Then if they should leave the tribe, the blood of that
woman will still be there in that tribe, a blood that is never forgotten;
it is a living thing.

Here, we demonstrate that the *Bombina* ROPA system can be used to measure changes in the production of proteins in transformed bacteria rather than using Western blots that only analyze protein level. Honesty time. I have some hoarding tendencies because I like collecting things. However, I do not really hoard, I really just want to keep certain things so that when I die, my house will smell nice and have a nice feeling. If I can’t have that anymore, I’m happy to have a place to keep important papers and important books.

The first thing I did was to write down all the things I had in my house that I could easily find any other place to store them in, just in case I die. I didn’t know quite how many there were, but the list was quite long – but then I found a really small book which had lists of things you need to keep on hand to survive (the UK government has one you can download) and that reduced the list significantly. My list now has two categories – items that I need for everyday life (not medical, not special) and items I will never use again. The thing I’ve realised is that if I want to keep something, it will always come back in some form – whether I’m using it, reusing it, or just coming across it.

The location of the July 15, 2002 flare is illustrated in Figure 1b. It is a bright, high elevation M7.5, class III solar flare and so was not observed by GOES or by SOHO, but was observed by RHESSI, on NASA’s Solar Terrestrial Relations Observatory (STEREO). These observations show that the flare was caused by a bright coronal jet that erupted out into open space, and then rotated away from the observer in the corona. The flare’s brightness and location were similar to the flare shown in Fig. 2a. One reason this flare has not been observed previously is that it occurred far away from active regions (ARs) on the sun, and even with the SOHO Solar and Heliospheric Observatory (SOHO), was not close to the east limb, where most flares erupt, but to the southeast, on the far side of the sun. These are the regions where RHESSI can produce its high angular resolution observations. Fig. 2b plots the brightness at the location and time that the flare occurred.

The RHESSI observations showed a bright coronal jet, seen in the Fe XXI emission line at 6.7 keV that is due to Fe XXI ions

text:
An inventory advertisement allows a continuous intake of applications, products, objects, etc. that exist in several locations in a company. One important aspect of the inventory is finding a desired (item) object using a predefined query.

-   *Inventory management* is based on the ability of handling data, to store information and to provide queries to the data. These tasks in particular also include the ability to define different data structures, to search for data within these data structures and to extract the required information for subsequent analysis.

-   *Information and knowledge management* is the management of information that contains information and knowledge. In general, all applications that support the business and make data management easier are defined as knowledge management systems.

Within the scope of this article the term *Data Management* refers specifically to the activities, which is undertaken by the data management systems. The following sections of this paper describe various methods for searching and for data access, for accessing distributed information, and for information exchange.

Searching for data
==================

text:
Was there recriticality at Fukushima Daiichi?

A:

Fukushima and Chernobyl are not at all the same types of reactors, and there is no "typical" nuclear event, despite the common media and blog mis-tagging of such things.  What they have in common is that in both cases they involved multiple reactor sites in one accident, the total combined power output of which exceeded the capacity of the backup reactor system, with one key difference. In both Chernobyl and Fukushima, the core(s) of the reactor(s) were melting for a time, releasing large amounts of heat.  At Chernobyl, this was due to a partial meltdown of the core, and at Fukushima the fuel rods were essentially melting within their containment vessels, but only for a few hours.
The only other important commonality was the large releases of radioactive materials.  At both Chernobyl and Fukushima, the radioactive release(s) were orders of magnitude greater than that observed at the more recent TMI events, or (more to the point) the recent coal-ash spill in Kingston, GA.  Note the recent coal ash spill in GA was not a nuclear reactor, but it was

On 24 September 2016, WorldPay, the payment provider, was re-branded 'Atradius', in line with the re-organisation of the Atradius Group.

History

Origins

The company was founded as Worldpay International, in the United Kingdom, in 1989, with the name Worldpay plc in 1993. The Worldpay brand was launched at the National Payments Conference in London in 1994, with the name Worldpay International Ltd. The following year the Worldpay brand was launched in the United States. In 1996, the company acquired Payment Corporation of America, a U.S.-based payment service. In 2002 the company changed its name from Worldpay PLC to Worldpay Group PLC. From January 2003, it was known as the Worldpay Group Holdings plc. In April 2004, Global Payments Europe (a subsidiary of Worldpay UK) was bought out by Worldpay International. In 2007, Royal Bank of Scotland, which had just acquired the credit card arm of British Gas, agreed to buy the company for approximately $US2.4 billion, but later backed out.

text:

How can I do this ?

A:

In a WebBrowser Object i get the HTML data and i am able to parse it and read the URL

Well, you should not ever need to do that. WebBrowser objects are inherently a horrible class -- not thread-safe, with no support for asynchronous loading of data, and not really capable to do that reliably.  If you need it, use the Internet Explorer control (if you want to be using Internet Explorer).  There's no reason to ever use the WebBrowser control.
You can write code to load any URL into a WebBrowser control, but that's going to be a bit of a kludge.  
The WebBrowser control's purpose (and its documented meaning) is to load Web content for display in the IE browser.  It does not let you inject arbitrary HTML into the page being displayed.

The EBS offers you the ability to create and mount a highly durable external storage volumes to your instances that can be accessed anywhere. To achieve this, you are given a block device formatted in a filesystem that is called Amazon EBS Volume. That volume can now be mounted at any mount point on your network.

In this post we will look at creating an EBS Volume of a given size (in GiB) and mount that to a node in an EC2 instance.

Step 1 – Create a new EBS Volume

Before you can create an EBS volume you need a storage capacity in GiB you can attach that volume to as per your needs.

The storage units, called EBS Volumes, are a single instance offering that is scalable up to 250 GiB of storage per volume (for a single EBS volume and that volume can be up to 500 GiB by the AWS storage standard). If your instance is an m1.small, the maximum volume size is 8GiB.

copytree copies the file or symlink. If destpath is a directory, alldirectory entries are copied.

-L, --chmod=[+|-]mode Change the file access mode for destdir and srcpath, accordingto mode (octal).

Do your knowledge workers need a shared call queue? For some companies, it's a no-brainer: It makes sense. There are many benefits. This post covers all of those. Here are my top four:

What you get: An easy way to share the workload during meetings. A shared queue lets users with the same skill set to discuss ideas and solutions and to make sure the important issues are on the main queue. This is especially helpful if a meeting has multiple participants, and the meeting organizer often ends up with many different types of information to distribute.

How it helps: It can help you ensure that key items are delivered in time for the next group meeting. It can help you distribute that one project member who isn't present at the meeting who often has insight about how to take the project home. And it gives you an easy way to let employees who normally have nothing to do at the end of a meeting know what tasks are next on the list, so those who work on them can move on.

prompt_prefix: Do your knowledge workers need a shared call queue?

Sugar And Our Kids: Are We Doing Enough?

Sugar has become a huge issue in health and safety in recent decades. High-sugar foods are linked to increased heart disease, strokes, and type 2 diabetes. And the evidence that high-sugar, high-fat diets are harmful continues to mount. One recent example: A study conducted at the University of Eastern Finland in 2012 with more than 2,500 adults in Finland found that those individuals whose diet included high amounts of sugar, salt and fat were more likely to have metabolic syndrome — which includes high blood pressure, high blood fat, abdominal fat and abnormal cholesterol.

The study was published in the journal The Lancet and concluded that people who consume diets that are high in sugar, salt and fat have a higher risk for developing metabolic syndrome than do those who eat foods high in protein, fruit and unsaturated fats.

So what is the problem?

Let’s take a look at a high-sugar, high-fat processed food commonly called “lunchables” (shown above) made by Quaker Oats to get a sense of how it works. It consists of an apple sauce with a can of mandarin orange juice topped with two pouches of apple

Low profile acoustic cabinet fans, manufactured from galvanized sheet steel, have been available for several years. These units have been installed in a wide variety of commercial applications, such as, offices, laboratories, manufacturing facilities, and data processors for several years. Low profile acoustic cabinet fans, however, have been used only with the standard 110 VAC, 60 Hz line frequency, single phase power source that has been used in the United States since before World War I. There are three problems associated with the use of low profile acoustic cabinet fans with such line frequency power sources.
The low profile acoustic cabinet fan has an airflow impedance which is about 1.3 times 10.sup.-4.sup.3 inches of water for a typical size fan. When such fans are mounted on a single phase 110 volts 60 Hz line having impedance of 1.3 to 7.5.times.10.sup.-5.times.10.sup.-4.sup.3 inches of water, they generate electrical noise at the low end of the audible frequency range. Furthermore, the low profile acoustic cabinet fan can produce excessive noise for some installations when operated at frequencies in the 1,200 Hz range. text:
Summary: An introduction to historiography and the history of ideas. An original synthesis of historiography with general studies of the history-making process and major ideas. A wide range of examples is used to demonstrate the principles of the historiographical method in its historical application: biography, biography and society, history and ideas, history and the sciences, history and religion, the history of culture and the history of historiography. This introductory text is designed as the first course work in a graduate-level history program and will be a useful source for advanced history students.

Instructor Description: Robert MacNair teaches U.S. History at Southern Methodist University. He received his BA from the University of Virginia and his MA and PhD from Rutgers University. Before joining the SMU faculty, he was a professor of history at the University of Kentucky.

Table of Contents: PART I: Historical Thinking 1. The Nature of Historical Thinking: An Exercise (15 pages) 2. I was brimming with excitement all of Thursday. When morning came, I was up and dressed in less than two minutes, eager to discover what new adventure had befallen me.

It was dark out. I looked out my window. Everything seemed covered with a thin layer of gray fog. Rain was falling, but not heavily yet.

"Come out into the garden and see the flowers that are blooming!" said Grandfather, as he stepped onto the porch in his bathrobe.

I took a deep breath and stepped outside. I sniffed the air warily as I looked all around.

"Everything looks so beautiful! What's so different?" I said.

"It's the blossoming of the flowers. There's nothing more exciting than seeing them blooming, isn't that so?" Grandfather pointed to the beds. Many of them were covered with white flowers and others with pink, crimson, and purple blossoms. "This year, there are more flowers than ever."
